senses.definition: a path for pedestrians at the side of a road
examples: She walked on the sidewalk to avoid the traffic.
The storefront has a bench on the sidewalk.

senses.definition: plural of sidewalk; pedestrian paths at the side of roads
examples: The sidewalks were crowded with tourists.
Keep to the sidewalks, please.

senses.definition: the wall on the side of a building or container
examples: The stickers were placed on the sidewall of the truck.
The brochure lists the measurements of the sidewall panels.

senses.definition: plural of sidewall; walls on the sides of a structure or container
examples: The tires touch the sidewalls poorly when underinflated.
Inspect the sidewalls for cracks.

senses.definition: to or toward the side; sideways
examples: She looked sideward to catch a glimpse.
The boat drifted sideward with the current.

senses.definition: in a sideward direction; sideways
examples: The car moved sidewards to avoid the obstacle.
She stepped sidewards to let him pass.

senses.definition: the water that washes to the side of something (often in maritime or engineering contexts)
examples: The sidewash from the propeller was noticeable.
Engineers accounted for sidewash in the design.

senses.definition: a position or movement to the side; a sideways direction
examples: He took a sideway step to avoid the obstacle.
The path runs to the sideway of the river.

senses.definition: to or along the side; not straight ahead
examples: The car moved sideways to fit through the gate.
She turned sideways to speak to him.

senses.definition: situated or moving toward the side
examples: A sideways glance
Sideways rain hit the window.

senses.definition: a wheel on the side of a machine or vehicle, often used for steering or propulsion
examples: The old ship had sidewheels instead of a tail.
Convert the sidewheel to an electric drive.

senses.definition: a vessel propelled by side wheels; a sidewheel steamer
examples: The riverboat was a famous sidewheeler from the 19th century.
Photographs show elaborate sidewheelers on the lake.

senses.definition: plural of sidewheeler; vessels or machines with side wheels
examples: Historical collections include several sidewheelers.
The museum featured multiple sidewheeler exhibits.

senses.definition: the wheels on the side of a vehicle or machine
examples: The antique clock has ornamental sidewheels.
Friction wore down the sidewheels of the gear system.

senses.definition: a weapon or object that moves to the side; also a slang term for a dangerous or unpredictable person or thing
examples: The missile model was named Sidewinder.
Watch out for that sidewinder in the negotiations.

senses.definition: plural of sidewinder; missiles or people/things that move erratically sideways
examples: The unit trained with several sidewinders.
The term is also used metaphorically for tricky individuals.

senses.definition: a swift brush or blow from the side; a sudden and superficial contact or effect
examples: The car received a sidewipe from the other vehicle.
The speech was a sidewipe to the main topic.

senses.definition: to strike or affect someone or something from the side
examples: A careless driver sidewiped the parked car.
The rumor sidewiped her reputation briefly.

senses.definition: a device or person that wipes or clears from the side; also a person who wipes windows from the side (informal) or a helicopter with sideward movement
examples: The sidewiper cleared the rain on the windshield.
In some markets, a sidewiper is used to remove debris from the side of a ship.

senses.definition: in a direction of the side; sideways
examples: The man swung his arm sidewise to block the punch.
A sidewise glance revealed nothing unusual.

senses.definition: a term from folklore referring to a boundary of the Otherworld; often found in Gaelic myth as a realm of fairies
examples: The travelers passed into the sidh where the fairies danced.
In the legend, the sidh is a liminal place between worlds.

senses.definition: a term used in certain traditions referring to a female seer or saint; often connected to yogic or tantric traditions in some contexts
examples: The sidha tradition emphasizes breath and inner energy.
He studied sidha practices under a master.

senses.definition: plural of sidha; practitioners or adherents of sidha traditions
examples: The sidhas gathered for a teaching session.
Historical texts mention several sidhas who achieved mastery.

senses.definition: the Gaelic supernatural beings, often translated as fairies or elves; a mythic realm or beings in Celtic folklore
examples: The sidhe are said to dance on moonlit nights.
Legends describe the sidhe as powerful, elusive beings.

senses.definition: a title used in some cultures for respected elders or spiritual leaders; sometimes used in nouns for mosques or religious figures
examples: The sidi spoke with quiet authority.
A sidi charms the crowd with stories.

senses.definition: a term appearing in certain linguistic or cultural contexts; may be a proper noun or specific term in some systems
examples: The scholar discussed sidia in the context of regional languages.

senses.definition: material applied to the exterior of a building for protection and decoration; the side covering of a wall
examples: The house has vinyl siding.
We chose cedar siding for the cottage.

senses.definition: to add siding to a building or to attach material to the side
examples: They will siding the exterior with aluminum panels.
The workers are siding the house this week.
